dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 1 for a sick person at a GRTC bus stop near Broad Street.
Hi, I'm Alex. I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I have pain in my head and chest. It's been going on for three hours now.
Can you tell me your name, please?
James.
What's the year and city today, Mr. James?
2020, Richmond.
What happened to bring you to emergency care today?
I was sitting here, had a few drinks, and it all started hurting-my head, chest, everywhere.
Are you having any trouble breathing?
No, I don't feel out of breath or anything like that.
Breath sounds are equal and clear bilaterally.
Can you tell me what your skin feels like right now?
It's normal, but feels cold and dry.
Blood pressure is 154 over 104.
Pulse is 76 beats per minute, strong and regular.
SpO2 is 96% on room air.
Can you describe the pain? Is it sharp, burning, pressure, or something else?
I don't really remember how this one feels exactly. It's everywhere-like a burning or pressure. It doesn't get worse when I move.

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: When did the pain start and was there anything specific that happened before it occurred?

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: It started when I started drinking. I had a few beers at the bus stop and then the pain came on all at once.
You mentioned back pain in your record before. Have you experienced anything like this pain before?
I remember one time when it was bad, like the worst I had ever experienced. It went away after a few hours, but I had trouble talking and walking for a while after.
When the pain started today, what time was it and what were you doing?
I'd say around three o'clock. I was here at the bus stop, had been drinking and the pain started as a sudden onset in my chest and grew to my head.
Does the pain get worse when you move or when you breathe? Does it go anywhere like your neck or arms?
No, I don't feel it get worse with movement. It doesn't reach my neck or arms, but it spreads to my right shoulder and back a little.
Would you describe the pain as sharp, dull, burning, or more like pressure?
Pressure, and tight, but not sharp.
Has it been all over your head and chest, or is it starting to focus in one area now?
It's still all over. Not focused in one spot.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst possible pain, how bad is it today?
I would say 5. It's bad, but not the worst.
We're going to perform some more tests to figure out exactly what's going on. Can you lie down for an EKG?
We're going to take a blood sugar reading now.
Your blood sugar is 94 mg/dL.

EKG results show an abnormal reading for a man your age: a large anterior and inferior infarct, age undetermined; possible right and left atrial enlargement, and T-wave changes consistent with lateral ischemia.
Applying a 4-lead cardiac monitor to keep checking for any rhythm changes or further issues.
Can you take a deep breath so we can reassess your lungs?
Sure.
Breathing is clear and lungs sound equal on both sides.
Any rashes, bumps, or other skin irregularities now visible?
No. Just my skin feels cold and dry.
Let's look at your eyes. Pupils are equal and reactive to light?
Yes, both equal and reactive. Head and neck present no trauma or abnormalities.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: Any soreness, drainage, or bleeding from ears, nose, or throat?

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Patient
dialogue.utterance: No. Everything in my face feels at rest.
Chest shows no deformities and breath sounds remain consistent bilaterally.
Movement is full in arms and legs.
No broken bones or signs of angulation to note.
EMS headquarters, we're initiating protocol 2-1 for chest pain with suspected cardiac issue.
Retaking vitals now: Pulse 87, Resp 16, BP 138/97, SpO2 98%, Blood Sugar 0 due to meter error, EKG shows sinus rhythm with some findings.
Retaking the 12-lead EKG, this time shows STEMI findings-possible acute elevation in the inferior and apical regions.
Mr. James, where would you like to go for care?
I want to go to McGuire VA Hospital. That's where I had the last heart test.
Securing you onto the stretcher now. Let's place you gently on the backboard.
Preparing the stretcher for the ambulance. Checking all straps and ensuring the board is secure for transport.
Ambulance is en route. All vitals and assessments are completed.
Calling McGuire now for arrival estimate and updating the ED with recent STEMI findings.
Transferring to bed 15 at McGuire VA Hospital. GCS 15.
Mr. James is alert and oriented to self and surroundings.
Repeat 12-lead EKG en route confirms STEMI findings-acute elevation in inferoapical areas.
EKG strips are with the notes. Vitals en route include BP: 138/97, P: 87, RESP: 16, O2: 98%, Blood Sugar Not Obtained.
Patient reported pain from head to chest, described as tight and pressure-like. History of possible prior STEMI in December 2019.
No medications currently, no allergies on file. Field impressions include Pain - Chest (non-cardiac) and Headache.
STEMI findings suggest revisit to diagnosis as Acute Myocardial Infarction.
Patient transported per protocol. Stability maintained during transport.
GCS 15, skin cold and dry, assessments complete.
Let me check your pain again-any changes in how it feels or what it's doing?
No, still pressure and tight. Still all over.
Any changes in how the pain started or progressed?
No, it was all at once when I started drinking. It's pretty much the same now.
Any new symptoms appear since we started working on you?
No. I have regular cold skin and no shortness of breath.
Final EKG en route confirms STEMI findings. Anterior and inferior infarction suspected.
Protocol 2-1 followed throughout. STEMI finding alters field impression to Acute Myocardial Infarction.
Preparing to hand over to hospital ED staff now.
Prior chest pain episode and reported infarct in 2019 noted.
Patient's request for hospital is McGuire VA Hospital with prior familiarity.
EKG strips, patient log, and field notes will be passed on to ED team at bed 15.
Bed 15 received. RN present. All interventions and EKG strips in folder.
Patient stable for STEMI care.
Patient is cooperative and preparing for ED evaluation.
No signs of trauma seen. Skin is cold and dry.
Glucose was 94 mg/dL, with backup not taken due to meter issue.
Pupils were equal and reactive at initial contact.
No respiratory distress observed.
Additional assessments as needed once in ED.
Vital signs during transport remain within normal limits with minor deviations.
No rashes, burns, or dermatological issues observed.
No trauma or signs of falls or incidents.
Patient's pain remains consistent throughout transport and assessment.
All interventions abortive; no medications administered during field care.
Patient declined field cardiac medications and no contraindications noted.
Transfer to ED complete.
Ambulance ready for next call after debriefing.
Patient moved to ED for further STEMI protocol.
All patient notes provided to ED as required.
Ambulance departed scene safely.
ED on notice for arrival with pre-arrival reports.
All prior medical history is included.
Debrief and citations due within 24 hours.
Ambulance clean and ready.
Patient received and admitted for triage.
EMS resources returned base for next mission.
